segments.s271-g137: This pre-verbal phrase sets scene and source. وز here is the fused poetic combination of و + ز (roughly “and/from/and then”), followed by آن پس (“after that”); ز ایران is the source prepositional phrase (“from Iran”), and سپه کرنای is a juxtaposed noun phrase meaning “the army’s karnā (trumpet)”. The whole segment is a fronted nominal phrase whose verb is supplied in the next segment.
segments.s271-g138: برآمد is the main past tense verb “arose/issued forth” (3rd‑singular). دم بوق is an idiomatic collocation: دم (“breath/blast”) + بوق (“trumpet/horn” or its blast) = “a blast of the trumpet.” و simply coordinates what follows, and هندی درای names the other sound/instrument (here ‘Indian …’), forming a coordinated object/description of the sounds that rose.
segments.s271-g113: برآرد is a literary 3rd‑person singular verb meaning 'brings forth' or 'springs up' (present/habitual or timeless poetic present). گل تازه 'fresh flower' follows the verb in inverted poetic order (verb first). از خار خشک is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the dry thorn' with از marking source and خشک as an adjective after its noun.
segments.s271-g114: شود is the 3rd‑person singular of شدن 'become' (here 'turns into'). خاک is the subject 'the earth/soil'. با بخت بیدار is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with (an) awakened fortune' (با + noun + adjective), and مشک is the predicate nominal 'musk' (i.e. 'the earth becomes musk'). The order is poetic (verb before subject/predicate), common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s271-g115: شگفتی‌تر is the comparative 'more astonishing' (adjective). آنک (classical/poetic form of آن‌که) is a relative/demonstrative 'that/which/who' introducing the clause. از پی means 'in pursuit of/after', so از پی آز مرد = 'a man who follows/pursues greed.' Word order is compact and literary.
segments.s271-g116: همیشه 'always' modifies the following verb phrase. دل خویش is 'his own heart' formed from خوی (root) + enclitic ش (here written separately) meaning 'own/his'; the particle ش is the possessive clitic 'his/her/its'. دارد is the present 'has/holds'. به درد literally 'into pain' = 'in sorrow' (به + noun used adverbially).
segments.s271-g117: میان 'between' introduces the two terms نیا (ancestor) and نبیره (descendant/scion). The coordinating و connects them; دو شاه 'two kings' specifies that both ancestor and descendant are kings (literal listing with poetic economy).
segments.s271-g118: ندانم is the negative present 'I do not know' (na‑ + دانم). چرا 'why' is the interrogative; باید here expresses necessity/obligation ('should / must be'), so باید این کینه‌گاه = 'why there should be this seat/place of hatred'. کینه‌گاه is a compound noun 'place/seat/occasion of hatred'.
segments.s271-g119: دو شاه و دو کشور lists 'two kings and two countries/realms' as the agents. چنین 'such/so' qualifies جنگجوی 'warriors' (a descriptive, somewhat exclamatory particle). The phrase is compact and nominative in structure, typical of epic catalogue lines.
segments.s271-g120: دو لشکر 'two armies/hosts' is the direct object. به روی اندر آورده is a verbal phrase 'have brought to (one another's) face' — آورده is the perfect participial/compound form 'brought', اندر is a classical preposition 'in/among', and the repeated روی (face) forms the idiom 'face to face' (i.e. brought the two armies to confront one another). The word order is poetic and compact.
segments.s271-g57: This clause uses an archaic/jussive verbal form 'مبیناد' (3rd-person jussive/subjunctive) meaning 'may [it] not be seen' or 'let not [anyone] see'. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: the verb 'مبیناد' precedes the noun phrase 'چشم کس آن روزگار' (literally 'eye of any/ no one that time'), so the whole means 'may no one's eye see that time.'
segments.s271-g58: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause ('that/when'). 'او' is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he'. 'پیشدستی' is a noun meaning the act or advantage of going first; together with the verb 'نماید' (3rd-person subjunctive/indicative here used as 'does/shows') it forms the predicate 'he takes the lead' (literally 'he shows/does precedence'). 'به کار' is a prepositional phrase 'in the affair / in the action' showing the domain of the initiative.
segments.s271-g59: 'که' again introduces the content clause 'that'. 'من خود' ('I myself') places emphasis on the speaker. 'بر آنم' is an idiom meaning 'I am resolved / fixed upon that' — 'بر' here is a prepositional element meaning 'on/about' and 'آنم' is 'I am of that' (classical style). 'کز' begins the subordinate content 'that/from which' and here functions as the conjunction 'that' introducing what the speaker is resolved to do. 'ایدر سپاه' means 'from here an army' (ایدر = here; سپاه = army).
segments.s271-g60: 'از آن سوی جیحون' is a prepositional phrase 'from the other side / across the Jeyhun' where 'سوی' here means 'side/direction'. 'گذارم' is a 1st-person singular verb meaning 'I will pass / I will cause to cross / I will lead across' (classical usage of گذارم for 'to cause to pass'). Finally 'به راه' ('by way of the road' / 'on the road') specifies the route. The clause states the action the speaker will carry out.
segments.s271-g61: This is a coordinated negative list introduced by repeated 'نه' ('neither / not'). 'نه گودرز مانم' literally 'I do not remain Goudarz' — here 'مانم' (1st-person present of ماندن) functions idiomatically as 'I am' in the negative sense ('I am not Goudarz'). The same pattern is repeated for the other proper names.
segments.s271-g62: Continuation of the negative list with inanimate nouns: 'گاه' (time/season), 'تاج' (crown), 'بوق' (horn/trumpet) and 'کوس' (war-drum). The repeated 'نه' negates each item; taken together the speaker denies being any of these persons or symbols.
segments.s271-g63: 'به ایران' ('into Iran') marks the destination; 'از آن گونه' ('in that manner' / 'in such a way') qualifies how the action will be done. 'رانم' is 1st-person present/future of راندن 'I will drive/lead' and 'سپاه' is its object 'an army' — together 'I will lead an army into Iran in that way.'
segments.s271-g64: 'کزان' ('from which' / 'by which') + 'پس' ('afterwards') introduce the result clause. 'نبیند کسی' is negative future ('no one will see'); word order puts the verb before the noun for emphasis. 'تاج و گاه' ('crown and throne/time/sovereignty') are the things nobody will see after that attack, expressing the intended destruction of royal symbols or rule.
segments.s271-g17: نبیند is a 3rd-person singular negative form (classical/present-general sense) meaning 'does not see' or 'has not seen'; here the verb precedes its subject (سپه), a common poetic word order for emphasis. چون + تو + سالار is a comparative phrase 'a commander like you' with چون introducing the comparison and تو specifying the person; نیز at the end ('also') links this clause to the parallel following clause.
segments.s271-g18: نبندد is 3rd-person singular negative (general/habitual) of بستن 'to bind' — here used idiomatically with کمر to mean 'gird (one's) loins'. The verb again precedes its object/subject phrase for poetic effect. کمر is the thing being 'bound' (the waist/girdle), چُـون تو هشیار is the comparative clause 'as you, vigilant' (چون + pronoun + adjective), and نیز repeats 'also' to keep the parallelism with the previous clause.
segments.s271-g19: ز introduces source/location ('from' — poetic use). تور و پشنگ are proper names (ancient lineages). ار is the conditional 'if' (archaic/poetic), placed before the verb. دراید is a classical-form verb meaning 'take/hold' or 'be moved to' (here 'affection takes/comes'); به مهر is the prepositional phrase 'toward/into affection' — together the clause means roughly 'if affection comes from Tur and Pashang'.
segments.s271-g20: چو تو پهلوان is a comparative phrase 'for/like a champion such as you' (چو = 'as/like', تو specifies the person, پهلوان = 'hero'). نیز again 'also/too' links to the conditional result. نارد سپهر is a poetic genitive-pattern phrase 'the spikenard of the heavens' (word order is inverted for effect); the verb sense is supplied by the previous conditional clause (i.e. 'then the heavens will send/emit [spikenard]').
segments.s271-g21: نخست is an adverb 'first' or 'at the outset'. آنک introduces reported material ('that which' / 'what'), and گفتی here functions as the 2nd-person past 'you said' (poetic form) introducing the reported remark; من از انجمن is the phrase 'I from the assembly' (از انجمن = 'in/from the assembly', marking the setting or source of the remark). Note: the supplied lemma senses for گفتی in the word list reflect 'conversation' (noun) and do not include the verbal past-2nd-person sense used here.
segments.s271-g22: گنهکار is a predicative noun/adjective 'sinful' or 'sinner'. دارم is the 1st-person present of داشتن used here in the sense 'I hold/consider (myself) to be' (transitive use: دارم + object/predicate). همی is a durative/emphatic adverb 'always/still', and خویشتن is the emphatic object 'myself'. The clause thus means 'I always consider myself a sinner.'
segments.s271-g23: که introduces a subordinate/time clause ('when' / 'that'). کیخسرو is the proper name (legendary king). آمد is the simple past 'came'. ز توران زمین is a source phrase 'from the land of Turan' (ز = 'from', زمین = 'land/country'), placed after the verb in classical poetic order.
segments.s271-g24: به ایران 'to Iran' marks the destination. و + با ما introduce the affected party/target ('and with us' — here idiomatically 'against us'). بگسترد is the classical perfect/past form of گستراندن 'to spread/extend' (3rd person), and کین is the object 'hatred/vengeance' — so the clause means 'and he spread hatred against us.'
segments.s271-g97: Word order: the object phrase 'سپه را' (the army + object marker را) precedes the verb; 'سراسر همه' is an intensifying modifier meaning 'entire/whole' that applies to the army; 'داد دل' is a verbal phrase 'gave (his) heart' with an implied subject ('he'). The 'را' here is the Persian definite/object marker marking 'سپه' as the recipient of the giving.
segments.s271-g98: 'که' introduces a purposive/result clause ('so that' / 'that'); 'از غم' = 'from sorrow' (از = from); 'بباشید' is a poetic/optative form meaning 'may be / be (made)'; here it expresses the wished-for/resulting state; 'آزاد دل' is the predicate 'a heart free (of sorrow)'.
segments.s271-g99: 'نهانی' is an adverb 'secretly/hiddenly' modifying the clause; 'روانش' = 'his soul/spirit' (روان + possessive suffix -ش); the construction 'پر از X بود' means 'was full of X'—here 'پر' + 'از' + 'درد' + 'بود' = 'was full of pain.' 'بود' is the past copula.
segments.s271-g100: Parallel predication: another 'پر از ... بود' phrase. 'پر از خون دل' = 'full of the blood of the heart' (poetic image); 'و' links the next noun phrase; 'بخت برگرد بود' = 'his fortune was turned (away/against him)' — 'بخت' = fortune/fate and 'برگرد' here describes a turned/unfavorable state, with 'بود' again as the past copula.
segments.s271-g101: 'که' here introduces a subordinate clause ('that / for'); 'از هر سوی' = 'from every side' (از = from, هر = every, سوی = side/direction); 'لشکر شهریار' = 'the king's army/host' (لشکر = army, شهریار = the king). Word order is typical: directional phrase before the noun it modifies.
segments.s271-g102: 'همی' is a classical prefix/particle indicating an ongoing or continual action (imperfective flavour); 'کاسته' is the verbal adjective/past participle 'diminished' (used here predicatively); 'دید' is the past-tense verb 'he saw / beheld' — together 'همی کاسته دید' = 'he saw it continually lessening'; 'در کارزار' = 'in the battle/field' (در = in, کارزار = battle).
segments.s271-g103: 'هم' = 'also'; 'از شاه خسرو' = 'because of / on account of King Khosrow' (از introduces the cause here); 'دلش بود تنگ' is an idiomatic predicate 'his heart was tight' = 'he was anxious/distressed' ('دل' + possessive -ش, 'بود' past copula, 'تنگ' = tight/narrow → anxious).
segments.s271-g104: 'بترسید' is the past-tense form 'he feared' (classical past of ترسیدن); it introduces the following subordinate clause beginning with 'کآید' ('که' + آید contracted) meaning 'that may come / would come'; 'یکایک' = 'one by one / each and every'; 'به جنگ' = 'to the fight / into battle'. The sequence expresses his fear that they would come one by one into battle.
segments.s271-g81: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. لشکر (subject) + به نزد تو آید is a standard old Persian word order: به نزد + pronoun + آید = 'comes near you'. مپای is an archaic negative imperative formed with م- (negative) + پای (step/stand) meaning 'do not move/advance'; here it attaches to the whole clause as an instruction given at that time.
segments.s271-g82: سر و تاج is a coordinated noun phrase 'head and crown' (direct objects). گودرز is the name in apposition. بگسل is a jussive/imperative-like form (b- prefix + verb) meaning 'tear off / sever'; ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from the place' specifying source/location.
segments.s271-g83: همان 'that/same' picks out a particular mountain. کو here is an archaic relative/locative 'where' (introducing the clause). کرده دارد حصار is a compound verbal line: کرده (made/done) + دارد (has) + حصار (a fortress) = 'which has made/has a fortress' (old poetic order where auxiliary follows participle).
segments.s271-g84: به اسبان جنگی is a prepositional phrase 'onto/against war-horses' (اسبان جنگی = 'war-horses'). ز پا اندر آر is an older verbal construction: ز پا 'from the feet' + اندر آر (compound verb with اندر + آر) = 'bring down / pull out from under their feet' — i.e. 'unhorse them'. The verb آر is an archaic root here meaning 'bring/put', used in a causative/unhorsing sense.
segments.s271-g85: مکش is an archaic negative imperative 'do not withhold / do not refrain'. دست از ایشان is the object phrase 'your hand from them' (دست = power/action). به خون ریختن is a verbal noun phrase 'in shedding blood' or 'from shedding blood' specifying the action you must not refrain from.
segments.s271-g86: تو پیروز باشی is an optative/jussive wish 'may you be victorious' with باشی in subjunctive. به + verbal noun (آویختن) expresses means/cause: 'by hanging' — i.e. 'may you win by hanging them' (آویختن used as the method of victory).
segments.s271-g87: ممان here is an imperative/negative root meaning 'do not spare/withhold' (i.e. 'do not leave alive'). زنده ز ایشان به گیتی کسی is an inverted, emphatic noun phrase: 'anyone of them alive in the world' (زنده = alive; به گیتی = in the world). The word order is emphatic/poetic: verb + predicate.
segments.s271-g88: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. نزد تو آید از ایشان بسی qualifies 'anyone' as 'who comes near you from among them'; بسی at the end is an adverb meaning 'many/much' or 'plenty', emphasizing their number. The clause restricts 'کسی' to those who approach you.
segments.s271-g73: This is a series of nouns introduced by the preposition ز (here meaning 'from'), listing sources of support: 'ز مردان' = 'from men/people', then 'وز گنج' is an archaic poetic pairing where وز functions as 'و ز' ('and from') joining 'گنج' ('treasure'), and 'نیروی دست' is a genitive phrase 'the strength/power of the hand' (i.e. manpower or manual force). Word order is nominal (preposition + noun phrases) as typical in Persian.
segments.s271-g74: همه ایزدی هرچ بایدت هست is a compact predicative clause: 'همه ایزدی' = 'all (that is) divine', 'هرچ' = 'whatever', and the verbal complex 'بایدت هست' literally 'is necessary for you' (باید + enclitic ت 'for you' + copula هست). Together: 'all divine things — whatever you need — exist/are present.' Note the poetically condensed order (adjectival/nominal phrase followed by the necessity copula).
segments.s271-g75: یکی نامور لشکری ده هزار: یکی 'one' (numeral/indefinite), نامور 'renowned' modifies لشکری ('military force/host'), and ده هزار gives the size 'ten thousand'. The noun phrase places the numeral and epithet before the head noun as common in Persian poetic diction.
segments.s271-g76: This is a sequence of adjectives joined by و describing that army: دلیر 'brave', خردمند 'wise'. گرد here is a terse/archaic term that contrasts with سوار; in context it pairs with سوار to indicate types of troops (an infantry/cavalry contrast), and سوار 'horseman' names the mounted element. The repeated و simply links the adjectives/terms.
segments.s271-g77: فرستادم اینک به نزدیک تو: فرستادم is a past tense verb 'I sent' (perfect/simple past), اینک 'now' is an adverb placed after the verb (poetic order), and به نزدیک تو is a prepositional phrase 'to your near/nearby place' = 'to your vicinity'.
segments.s271-g78: که روشن کند جان تاریک تو: که introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that'/'in order that'), روشن is an adjectival/predicative root 'bright/clear' used with کند 'may make' (کند = present/subjunctive form of 'to do/make') to mean 'may make bright', جان تاریک تو = 'your dark soul' (possessive order: noun + adjective + pronoun). The verb form کند expresses purpose/optative in classical Persian.
segments.s271-g79: از ایرانیان ده وز اینها یکی: از ایرانیان ده = 'ten of the Iranians' (a numeric partitive), then وز اینها یکی is an archaic coordinated clause meaning 'and of these one' (وز should be read as the poetic contraction و ز = 'and from/of'). The construction states a ratio (ten to one) between Iranians and 'these' people.
segments.s271-g80: به چشم یکی ده سوار اندکی: به چشم 'to the eye' = 'in appearance', followed by a comparative phrasing: 'one (appears) [as] ten horsemen' (ده modifies سوار in the line though 'ده' is not listed among the segment word entries), and اندکی 'few/a little' comments that even that apparent strength is small. Word order puts the idiom 'به چشم' first, then the comparative image and the qualifier.
segments.s271-g105: 'به یزدان' is a prepositional phrase 'to God' (به + noun). 'چنین گفت' = 'thus said' where گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (3rd sg.) following the manner adverb چنین — a normal poetic word order (manner before verb). 'کای کردگار' is a vocative address 'O, Creator' (کای is an archaic/poetic vocative particle); the vocative follows the reporting clause and introduces the direct address that follows in the next segment.
segments.s271-g106: چه مایه is an exclamatory phrase meaning 'what (a) ...' (چه = exclamative, مایه = cause/measure). شگفت here functions as a noun/adjective 'wonder/strangeness'. 'اندر این روزگار' = 'in these times' (اندر is an archaic/pre-literary preposition 'in/within'; این روزگار = 'this age/these days'). Note there is no copula; Persian omits 'is'.
segments.s271-g107: 'که را' is the accusative/question form 'whom' (که + object marker را). 'برکشیدی' is the past tense 'you raised/exalted' (2nd person past). The overt subject 'تو' follows the verb (poetic emphasis). 'افگنده نیست' consists of the past-participle/adjunct افگنده 'cast down' used predicatively with the negative copula نیست 'is not' — so the clause states a stative condition about the person mentioned (i.e. 'whom you raised is not cast down' / 'none is cast down').
segments.s271-g108: جز از تو ... نیست = 'there is none except you ...' where جز از تو marks an exception 'except from you'. 'جهاندار دارنده' is a doubled title in apposition (two nouns meaning 'world-holder / possessor' or 'sovereign, possessor'), followed by the negative copula نیست 'is not' to assert non-existence of any such being other than the addressee. Word order places the exception phrase before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s271-g109: 'به خسرو نگر' is an imperative 'look at Khosrow' (به + proper name + نگر (imperative of نگاه/نگریدن)). 'تا' here introduces a reason/explanatory clause ('for' / 'since' / 'that'), and 'جز از کردگار' = 'apart from the Creator' (جز از = except from/besides), setting up the restriction for the following rhetorical question.
segments.s271-g110: که دانست کآید یکی شهریار = 'who knew that a king would come'. که here is a relative/complementizer 'that/who'. دانست is past tense 'knew' (3rd sg.). کآید is an older/poetic subjunctive/future-form 'that may come / would come' (کَ + آید contracted), and 'یکی شهریار' = 'one / a certain king' (یکی = one/a certain + شهریار = king).
segments.s271-g111: 'نگه کن' is an imperative 'look!' (نگه = gaze/watch, کن = do — the imperative auxiliary). 'بدین کار' = 'at/with this deed' (بدین = به + این, a contracted/prepositional + demonstrative meaning 'at this' or 'with this', and کار = deed/affair). 'گردنده دهر' is an epithet 'turner of the age/world' where گردنده is a present-participle used as agent 'turner' and دهر = 'age, time, the world' — together 'the turner of Time' (an epithet for Fate or God).
segments.s271-g112: 'مر' = 'to me' (old-fashioned dative 'me') placed before the clause for emphasis. 'آن را که' = 'that which' (آن = that, را = object marker, که = relative 'which/that'). 'از خویشتن کرد بهر' = 'he did for his own sake' (از خویشتن = 'from/of himself' = for himself, کرد = past 'did/made', بهر = 'for / for the sake of'). The relative clause follows its antecedent normally; the whole clause is a request to be told that action.
segments.s271-g121: چه گویی is a rhetorical interrogative: چه (what) + گویی, here functioning like the archaic/poetic present 'you say' or 'one says' (literally 'do you say' / 'shall one say'), asking for an opinion. سرانجام این کارزار is a noun phrase 'the final outcome / end of this battle' (سرانجام = outcome; این کارزار = 'this battle'), placed after the question head as the thing being asked about.
segments.s271-g122: This clause is a free relative / indirect question introduced by که (who/whom) with the object marker را following it; 'که را' = 'whom'. برکشد is a poetic, prefixed form of a verb (here meaning 'to strike down / slay' in context) and گردش روزگار is the nominative/agent phrase 'the turning/wheel of the times/fate' that performs the action. Word order is object (که را) + verb (برکشد) + agent (گردش روزگار), a common flexible order in older poetic Persian.
segments.s271-g123: پس آنگه is a double temporal adverbial ('then' / 'after that') emphasizing sequence. به یزدان بنالید زار is a verb phrase headed by بنالید (past/epic past 'he wailed/lamented') with indirect object به یزدان ('to God') and the adverbial or predicative زار ('bitterly / in anguish'); poetic Persian frequently places the adverb after the verb as here.
segments.s271-g124: که‌ای begins an address: که (here functioning as a vocative particle combined with ای, rendered as 'O') calling on God; the following three nouns are epithets in apposition: روشن (bright/enlightened), دادگر (just), کردگار (Creator/God). In classical poetry epithets pile up after a vocative in this compact form.
segments.s271-g125: گر is the conditional 'if'. افراسیاب is the subject (proper name). اندر این کینه‌گاه is a locative phrase 'in this kincah (place/seat of hatred)' — اندر (in) + این (this) + کینه‌گاه (place of hatred); the whole forms the subordinate conditional clause's setting.
segments.s271-g126: ابا is an older verb meaning 'refuse / be reluctant' used here intransitively about Afrasiab's attitude; the following nominal group نامداران توران سپاه is an object-like / topic phrase 'the famed men of Turan's host' (نامداران = renowned ones + توران = Turan + سپاه = host/army). The clause is elliptical (the consequences are implied or continue in the next line).
segments.s271-g127: بدین رزمگه is a locative prepositional phrase formed from بدین ('by/in this') + رزمگه ('battlefield'). کشته خواهد شدن is a future passive construction: کشته (past participle 'killed') + خواهد (future auxiliary 'will') + شدن (passive infinitive 'be'), together 'will be killed'. Note that older Persian often stacks participles and auxiliaries in this order.
segments.s271-g128: This clause repeats a future passive construction with سربخت ما as the affected phrase. سربخت (here a poetic/archaic noun relating to head/pride/fate) + ما (our) is the patient/topic; گشته (perfect participle 'become/turned') + خواهد شدن (future passive 'will be') again produces a future passive sense ('our ... will be [made/turned]'). The doubling of participle + خواهد شدن is a poetic intensification common in epic diction.
segments.s271-g89: This is a compact past-tense clause with subject–verb–object implied in Persian order: فرستاده (subject) + بشنید (3rd-person past ‘he heard’) + پیغام شاه (object, 'the king's message'). Poetic diction omits an overt conjunction; the noun phrase پیغام شاه is the definite object of بشنید.
segments.s271-g90: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came'. بر here is the directional preposition 'to/toward' and governs the following noun phrase; پهلوان سپاه is a genitive grouping 'the champion of the army'. The clause continues the narrative (subject is the messenger carried over from the previous clause).
segments.s271-g91: به پیش اندر آمد is a multi-word locative phrase: به پیش ('to/into the presence of') + اندر آمد ('entered/came in'). In poetry such particles stack; the verb آمد (past) closes the clause. به سان شمن is a simile ('in the manner of / like a shaman/sorcerer').
segments.s271-g92: خمیده چو ... is a comparative descriptive clause: خمیده (adjective 'bent') + چو ('as/like') introducing the comparison. از بار شاخ سمن is a prepositional phrase 'as if from the burden/load (بار) of a jasmine (سمن) bough (شاخ)', where از marks the source/cause. Word order is adjective → comparative particle → explanatory prepositional phrase.
segments.s271-g93: This clause uses the dative/goal marker به + indirect object: به پیران رسانید پیغام شاه = 'he conveyed/delivered the king's message to the elders.' رسانید is past-tense causal/transitive 'conveyed/delivered' and پیغام شاه is the direct object.
segments.s271-g94: وز آن نامداران جنگی سپاه is a terse poetic phrase. Here وز functions as a contracted poetic particle (literary 'و ز' ≈ 'and/of' or a variant of از), introducing an additional group 'آن نامداران جنگی سپاه' = 'those famed warlike men of the host/army'. This is an appositive/coordinate addition to the previous dative.
segments.s271-g95: چو بشنید introduces a temporal 'when' clause ('when they heard'). پیران is the subject of this clause. سپه را uses the object-marker را to mark سپه ('the army') as the direct object, and بخواند is a past-tense hortatory/indicating verb here meaning 'called/summoned' (third-person). Note the verb follows its object as is common in Persian.
segments.s271-g96: This is a second 'when' clause: فرستاده (the messenger) is the subject; چون این سخن ('when/when he heard this speech/these words') is temporal; باز راند is a compound verbal phrase where باز ('back/again') modifies راند ('drove/turned away'), so the messenger 'turned/drove back' upon hearing the words. The verb is past tense and the clause is elliptical in typical epic narration.
segments.s271-g41: همیشه چنینست is a comment-clause meaning “it is always thus”; کار نبرد is a nominal phrase ‘the affair/work of battle’ with کار (the affair) followed by the modifier نبرد (battle). The clause states a general truth before the following clause specifies what “this” is.
segments.s271-g42: ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase ‘from every side’; همی before the verb گرد/گردد is the archaic/higher-register habitual marker (classical Persian) giving a recurring present sense; گردد (گشت#گرد) here means ‘turns/occurs’; این تیره گرد is the syntactic subject/predicate-nominal following the verb (verb–subject order is common in older Persian) and describes the ‘dark/ominous turning’ that comes from every side.
segments.s271-g43: گهی marks ‘sometimes’ and introduces an alternating clause. برکشد (بر + کشد) is a present/habitual third-person form meaning ‘raises/lifts up’; تا here gives the extent ‘as far as/up to’; به خورشید سر literally ‘to the sun’s head’ (به + خورشید + سر), with سر ‘head/top’ used poetically. Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrase that expresses its extent.
segments.s271-g44: The second گهی begins the opposed alternate. اندر آرد is literally ‘brings/puts in’ (آرد = brings); with ز خورشید بر the phrase means motion away from the sun – i.e. ‘brings back / draws away from the sun’. Classical word order and particles (اندر, ز … بر) express source and direction compactly rather than with a modern analytic phrasing.
segments.s271-g45: به یکسان is an adverbial phrase ‘in the same way/identically’; نگردد (negative of گشتن) means ‘does not become / does not remain (the same)’; سپهر بلند is the subject ‘the high/lofty sky’ placed after the verb (common in older Persian), so the clause says the sky is not uniform or constant.
segments.s271-g46: Repeated گهی … گهی signals alternation. شاد دارد literally ‘(it) has/keeps joyful’ — here داشتن is used impersonally of the sky: ‘sometimes it is/causes joy’; در the second half مستمند is a predicate meaning ‘needy/afflicted’. The subject (سپهر بلند) from the previous clause is implied for both predicates.
segments.s271-g47: گهی ‘sometimes’ opens another alternating predication. با + noun is the instrumental/comitative ‘with’; here the clause says the (same) subject accompanies or is accompanied ‘with wine and with entertainers’. In classical usage the verb ‘to go/flow’ can be implied or given by رود — in context رود functions as a verbal/nominal element indicating movement with the listed companions; رامشگران is a plural agent ‘comforters/entertainers’ and is coordinated to می (wine).
segments.s271-g48: Again گهی begins the alternate: با غم و گرم و با اندهان lists companions/accompaniments. با غم ‘with sorrow’; گرم here is an adjective ‘ardent/fervent’ modifying the accompanying mood; the final با + اندهان (‘with thoughts/intentions’) uses an older noun-form (اندهان) to indicate mental accompaniment. The coordination (و) strings these states together as alternatives to the wine-and-entertainers scenario.
segments.s271-g129: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' (archaic/common classical usage). کیخسرو آید is a simple clause: proper name (subject) + present/future verb 'آید' meaning 'comes' (the verb follows the name). ز ایران uses the poetic preposition 'ز' = 'from' to mark origin. به کین expresses purpose/intent with به + abstract noun 'کین' = 'for vengeance' ('to take vengeance').
segments.s271-g130: بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him/unto him' (short form). بازگردد is a 3rd-person verb here meaning 'will be returned / will be restored' (an intransitive/passive sense: something comes back to someone). سراسر زمین is the noun phrase 'the whole land' (سراسر 'entire', زمین 'land'); word order places the verb before this phrase for emphasis, so the reading is 'the entire land will be restored to him.'
segments.s271-g131: روا باشد is an optative/formulaic phrase: 'it is fitting / it would be right' (روا = fitting/permissible; باشد = 'be' in the optative/3rd-person modal sense). ار is the archaic conditional 'if'. خسته is a predicate adjective 'weary/tired'. در جوشنم = 'in my cuirass/armor' (در = in; جوشن with the suffix -م = 'my'). The clause sets a conditional circumstance ('if I am worn in my armor').
segments.s271-g132: برآرد is a 3rd-person verb in the optative/subjunctive sense 'may (he) bring forth / take away' (here: 'that [the Creator] take out'). روان is 'the soul/spirit' (direct object). کردگار is the agent 'the Creator/God' (subject of this verb). از تنم = 'from my body' (از = from; تنم = my body). Note: تنم is the possessed noun 'my body' in sense, but the provided sense list did not give a matching 'body' sense, so that mapping is handled below.
segments.s271-g133: مبیناد is an optative/subjunctive form from 'بین/مبین' meaning 'appear/see' (here 'may ... see/come to light'); with هرگز ('never') it yields 'may never ... be seen/perceived'. جهانبین is a compound 'world-seer' (literally one who sees or judges the world; i.e. a worldly-minded observer); من attached here functions as 'of mine'/'as for me' (classical word order puts the possessive pronoun after the noun). So the whole phrase is 'May my/worldly observer never perceive...' (i.e. 'may it never be perceived, by worldly eyes, that...').
segments.s271-g134: گرفته is a past-participial adjective 'seized / taken' (here forming a perfect sense 'has taken'). کسی is the indefinite 'someone / anyone' as the logical subject of گرفته. راه و آیین من = 'my way and my custom/rule' (راه = path/way; آیین = practice/ordinance; من = my). The clause therefore states the content that should never be perceived: 'that anyone has seized my way and my ordinance.'
segments.s271-g135: که را is the relative/conditional 'for whom' (که = who/that; را is the classical/poetic object/topic marker used with the relative). گردش روز = 'the turning/course of the day' (گردش = circuit/course; روز = day/time/fortune). با کام نیست = 'is not with (his) desire' — با + کام = 'in harmony with wish/fortune', نیست = 'is not'. Together: 'for whom the day's course is not as he desires.'
segments.s271-g136: ورا is an emphatic/dative form 'for him / to him' (archaic particle used to mark the person concerned). زندگانی = 'life' (existence/living). و = 'and'. مرگش = 'his death' (مرگ + possessive -ش). یکیست = 'is one' / 'are one' (predicate stating identity or equivalence). So the clause states: 'for him, life and his death are one.'
segments.s271-g65: به کیخسرو این پس نمانم جهان: 'به کیخسرو' is a dative/benefactive phrase 'for/to Kay Khosrow' (here meaning 'with respect to Kay Khosrow'); 'این پس' is a temporal phrase 'from now on / henceforth'; 'نمانم' is 1st person singular negative present (classical Persian form used for future sense) 'I will not remain'; 'جهان' is the noun 'the world / this life' and is the domain in which the remaining would occur.
segments.s271-g66: به سر بر فرود آیمش ناگهان: 'به سر' = 'upon the head' (به + سر, instrument/target); 'بر' is the directional/locative particle used with the verb here ('to descend/come upon'); 'فرود آیمش' is a compound verb meaning 'I shall come down / descend upon him' — the final -ش is the enclitic 3rd-person object pronoun 'him/it' referring back to Kay Khosrow; 'ناگهان' is the adverb 'suddenly'.
segments.s271-g67: به خنجر از آن سان ببرم سرش: 'به خنجر' uses به instrumentally ('with a dagger'); 'از آن سان' is an adverbial phrase 'in that way / thus' (از + آن + سان); 'ببرم' is 1st person present (classical) used as future 'I will cut/take off' (here contextually 'cut off'); 'سرش' = 'his head' (سر + enclitic possessive ش).
segments.s271-g68: که گرید بدو لشکر و کشورش: 'که' introduces the result/purpose clause 'so that / so as'; 'گرید' is 3rd-person present (classical) of گریستن, 'will/shall weep' (used to indicate what will happen); 'بدو' = 'for him/to him' (به + او fused), giving the beneficiary of the weeping; 'لشکر و کشورش' is the subject 'his army and his country' (لشکر = army, کشورش = his country).
segments.s271-g69: مگر کآسمانی دگرگونه کار: 'مگر' here, combined with the following 'که' (written attached to آسمانی), functions as 'unless'; 'آسمانی' is an adjective 'heavenly' describing the following noun; 'دگرگونه کار' = 'a different affair/deed' ('دگرگونه' = different, 'کار' = matter/act).
segments.s271-g70: فراز آید از گردش روزگار: 'فراز آید' = 'come forth / arise on high' (فراز = on high/top; آید = comes/happens); 'از گردش روزگار' = 'from the turning/circuit of روزگار' — گردش = rotation/turning, روزگار here means 'fate / the fortunes of time', so the phrase means an event arising out of change in fortune.
segments.s271-g71: ترا‌ای جهاندیده سرافراز: 'ترا' is the object/dative form used vocatively here 'O you'; 'ای' is the vocative particle addressing the addressee; 'جهاندیده' is an epithet 'worldly‑wise / experienced in the world'; 'سرافراز' is another epithet 'lofty / honored', both stacked as direct address.
segments.s271-g72: نکردست یزدان به چیزی نیاز: 'نکردست' is a classical perfect/past form 'has not done / has not made'; the logical subject 'یزدان' (God) follows the verb in inverted poetic order; 'به چیزی نیاز' = 'in need of anything' (به + چیزی marking the thing of need). Together: 'God has not made you in need of anything.'
segments.s271-g33: نباشم is a first‑person negative/desiderative form (old/poetic) meaning roughly “let me not be / I am not.” همیدون is a proper name/epithet placed after the verb as predicate: the clause reads “I am not Hemidun.” او را نیا is an imperative negative addressed to a second person: او (he) with را (definite/object marker) plus نیا (don’t bring/come) gives “do not bring him (to me).” Word order is verb → predicate → (imperative clause).
segments.s271-g34: نجویم is the first‑person form of ‘seek’ (here with the negative prefix n‑): “I will not seek” or “I do not seek.” همی is the old adverb used with verbs (here giving continuity/emphasis: “still/indeed”). ز این سخن means “from/of these words,” and کیمیا (used figuratively) means “the elixir/secret (of alchemy)” — object of the seeking. The whole clause means “I will not seek the elixir from these words.”
segments.s271-g35: بدین کار is the prepositional phrase ‘in/with this affair’ (بدین = به + این). او is the third‑person pronoun ‘he.’ کس گنهکار نیست is a negated predicate: کس here functions as ‘anyone/no one’ and گنهکار ‘guilty/sinner,’ with نیست negating the copula — ‘no one is guilty’ or more naturally here, ‘he is not guilty in this matter.’
segments.s271-g36: مرا با جهاندار پیکار نیست is an existential/possessive‑feeling construction: مرا marks the experiencer/object (‘for me’ / ‘I’), با جهاندار ‘with the sovereign,’ پیکار ‘battle/strife,’ and نیست ‘is not / there is not.’ Word order places the experiencer and prepositional phrase before the negated predicate, yielding ‘I have no battle with the sovereign’ or ‘I am not at war with the king.’
segments.s271-g37: چنین بود is a simple past copula clause: چنین ‘thus/so’ + بود ‘was.’ و is the conjunction ‘and.’ این بودنی is a nominalized participle (‘this being’ / ‘that which is’), and کار بود places کار ‘work/affair’ with بود ‘was’ as predicate: “and this being was (the) work/nature (of it).” The sequence ties a statement of fact (چنین بود) to a characterization of its nature.
segments.s271-g38: مرا از تو در دل چه آزار بود is an old‑style experiential question. مرا marks the experiencer (‘to me’ / ‘for me’), از تو ‘because of/from you,’ در دل ‘in (my) heart,’ چه آزار ‘what harm/affliction,’ and بود ‘was.’ Literal order is experiencer → source → locus → question phrase → copula, yielding “What harm was there in my heart because of you?”
segments.s271-g39: و دیگر که گفتی ز کار سپاه begins a turn in the narrative: و دیگر ‘and besides/furthermore’; که introduces the reported clause (‘that/when/which’); گفتی is ‘you said’ (old narrative/statement of speech); ز کار سپاه ‘about the affair of the army/host’ — ‘when you spoke of the army’s business.’
segments.s271-g40: ز گردیدن تیره خورشید و ماه: ز is the poetic/genitive preposition ‘from/because of’; گردیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning ‘to become/turn’ (here ‘the becoming’ or ‘darkening’); تیره modifies that verbal noun (‘dark/ominous’); خورشید و ماه ‘sun and moon.’ Together: ‘from the darkening/turning (i.e. eclipse/omen) of the sun and moon.’
segments.s271-g25: بدین here (= به + دین in classical/poetic spelling) functions as a discourse adverb meaning 'in this way / by this'; من که شاهم is a relative clause 'I, who am king' (شاهَم = 'I am king' with the clitic -م). نیازرده‌ام is a perfect (first‑person) form meaning 'I have been reduced to need/begging' or 'I have supplicated' (poetic/compound verbal form). The clause says the speaker, as king, has not been brought to that state.
segments.s271-g26: به دل is a prepositional phrase 'into/within the heart'; هرگز is the negative adverb 'never' that scopes over the whole following nominal; این یاد is the object 'this remembrance/thought'; ناورده‌ام is a negative perfect formed from نا‑ + آورده‌ام (poetic contraction) meaning 'I have not brought/ admitted'. Word order is poetic (prepositional phrase + negator + object + verb).
segments.s271-g27: نباید is an impersonal modal 'should not / must not'; که introduces a subordinate clause and باشی is the second‑person present/subjunctive form 'you be' (classical/subjunctive use after نباید). بدین modifies تنگدل: 'in this way narrow‑hearted' (بدین = 'so/in this manner', تنگدل = 'narrow‑hearted/close‑minded').
segments.s271-g28: ز تیمار is an ablative/causal phrase 'from (or because of) care/anxiety'; یابد is a third‑person present/future 'will find' (poetic order here); ترا = 'you' as the object (ت + را); زنگ دل 'the bell/alarum of the heart' is the grammatical subject placed at the end for emphasis: 'the heart's alarm will find you.' The inverted order is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s271-g29: که is a connective 'that/which'; آن بودنی is a nominalized participle 'that‑which‑is / the existent' (poetic expression for 'existence'); بود is the past copula 'was'; از کردگار = 'from the Creator/God' (agent/source). The clause asserts the origin of being.
segments.s271-g30: نیامد is past negative 'did not come'; بدین 'in this way'; بد کس is the noun phrase 'an evil/bad person' (بد modifying کس); آموزگار 'teacher' appears as the final noun (subject) — overall: 'no evil teacher came in this manner.' Word order is compressed and emphatic, typical of epic verse.
segments.s271-g31: که introduces a purposive/causal clause 'so that / lest'; کیخسرو is the subject; از من 'from me'; نگیرد is the negative present/subjunctive/optative form 'he not take' (ن‑ + گیرد), here marking prevention or fear; فروغ 'radiance' is the object. So the clause expresses 'lest Keykhosrow should take my radiance.'
segments.s271-g32: نبیره is the noun 'scion/offspring/heir' (object label); مخوانش is an imperative/negative‑imperative‑styled verbal phrase meaning 'do not call/name him' in the poem's compressed diction (m‑ prefix marks the verbal form + خوانش stem + ـش 'him'); که باشد دروغ is a subordinate clause 'for that would be false' with باشد subjunctive 'it be' and دروغ 'a lie'. The line forbids naming him heir because that would be untrue.
segments.s271-g49: Word order is subject-object-verb: تو (you) is the addressed subject, دل را is the definite object (را marks the direct object). بدین is the classical contraction of به + این (“by/with this” or “with such”), and خسته is an adjective meaning “weary/tired” applied to دل. مدار here is an archaic/poetic imperative form meaning “do not make/do not cause” (negative imperative sense), so the whole clause literally reads “You — do not make the heart weary with this pain.”
segments.s271-g50: Again object-marking with را: روان را = “the soul/spirit” (object). بدین کار = “by/with this affair” (بدین = به + این). بسته is a participial/adjectival form from بستن meaning “bound/tied” (so روان را ... بسته = “bind the soul”), and مدار is the same archaic imperative “do not make/do not bind.” The clause therefore says “Do not bind the soul to this affair.”
segments.s271-g51: سخن گفتن is a verbal-noun/gerund phrase meaning “talking/speaking” or “the talk (about...)”; کشتگان is in genitive/qualification relation ("of the slain" or "about the slain"). گشت is the past verb “became/turned (into)” and خواب is the noun “sleep/drowsiness” used metaphorically. The clause reads literally “Talking about the slain became sleep,” i.e. speaking of the dead is like sleep/dulls one or has grown to be mere lament.
segments.s271-g52: ز introduces a causal/ablative phrase “because of/from,” here ز کین برادر = “because of a brother’s hatred/vengeance.” تو is the vocative/subject “you.” سر برمتاب is a verbal phrase: سر (“head”) + برمتاب, an imperative-form verb meaning roughly “turn/attack/strike” (برمتاب = set/turn upon / assail). Grammatically the prepositional cause phrase (ز کین برادر) gives the motive/reason for the imperative addressed to تو.
segments.s271-g53: This is a relative clause introduced by the archaic کو (= that/which) following the noun: دلی کو = “a heart which/that.” ز introduces the cause “from/because of,” so ز درد برادر = “from the pain for/of a brother.” شخود is an archaic/reflexive/form related to خود (itself); here it refers back to the heart. The clause therefore describes “a heart which, because of a brother’s pain, has (been) itself (consumed/affected).”
segments.s271-g54: علاج پزشکان is the topic “the physicians’ remedies.” نداردش is composed of ندارد (“does not have/there is not”) plus the enclitic pronoun ش (referring back to دلی), so syntactically it means “the doctors’ remedy has no effect on it” or “the physicians have no remedy for it.” سود is the noun “benefit/profit” and completes the negated idea: “no benefit.”
segments.s271-g55: سه دیگر = “three others” (the numeral + 'other(s)'); که گفتی is an indirect-speech marker “that you said” (گفتی is the 2nd-person past tense “you said”). The following که introduces the reported clause about خسرو (Khosrow) and پگاه (dawn), so this segment sets up an item enumerated by the speaker: “three others that you mentioned — that Khosrow at dawn ...”
segments.s271-g56: به جنگ اندر آید is a common classical collocation meaning “to come/enter into battle”: به + جنگ (to war) + اندر (into/within) + آید (comes; here a present used to indicate an imminent/future action). همی is an archaic adverb/verb‑particle that marks the verbal aspect (common in Ferdowsi) and emphasizes the action’s actuality/immediacy. با سپاه is an instrumental/ accompaniment phrase “with an army/host.” Altogether: “will go/enter into battle at dawn with an army.”
segments.s271-g1: This is a temporal/conditional clause: prepositional phrase + simile + verb. به introduces the following noun phrase; چون 'like/as' links تاج (crown) as a simile; تاج بر سر نهاد is a compact past clause with the verb نهاد (past of نهادن, 'to place') at the end — Persian SOV order. نهاد is 3rd‑person past, so the whole segment reads '(he) placed ... on (his) head.'
segments.s271-g2: همانگه is an adverb meaning 'at that same moment.' فرستاده را is the definite direct object (را marks the object). در here is the noun 'door' and گشاد is an adjective 'open/gaping'; poetry often omits an overt verb (e.g., گشود) so در گشاد with the object implies 'he opened the door to the messenger.'
segments.s271-g3: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past meaning 'he commanded/said.' تا introduces a subordinate clause giving purpose or the content of the order and takes a subjunctive-like form, so بازگردد is read as 'should return' (3rd person subjunctive/present form used for a commanded action). به جای marks the destination 'to (his) place.'
segments.s271-g4: سوی is the preposition 'toward/to.' نامور is an attributive adjective 'renowned' modifying بنده; the sequence بنده کدخدای should be read as a possessive/genitive relation: 'the servant of the کدخدا (village‑headman).' In Persian poetry the ezāfe or linking particle is often dropped, so the whole phrase means 'toward the famed servant of the kadkhoda.'
segments.s271-g5: چنین sets the manner 'thus/so.' پاسخ آورد is a past tensed expression 'he brought/gave an answer' (آورد used as 'reported/said'). کو را بگوی functions as the lead‑in to direct speech: بگوی is a jussive/imperative‑like form 'say' (introducing what is to be said), را marks the following content as definite. Note: کو here is an archaic/poetic particle introducing what is to be said (see explanation), so the construction roughly means 'he answered thus:
segments.s271-g6: که‌ای is the vocative particle made of که + ای (classical poetic address) meaning 'O' before addressing someone. The following three words are epithets in series: مهربان 'kind', نیکدل 'good‑hearted', راستگوی 'truthful' — all attributive adjectives used vocatively to address the addressee. This segment introduces who is being spoken to.
segments.s271-g7: تو is the vocative 'you.' تا here has a temporal/limit sense ('as/when' or 'being'), introducing the clause about birth/lineage; زادی means 'noble birth/lineage.' از مادر links that lineage to 'from (a) mother.' پاکتن is an archaic/poetic adjective meaning 'pure' describing مادر. The phrase literally says 'you, being of noble birth from a pure mother.'
segments.s271-g8: سرافراز بودی is a past‑tense copular clause: سرافراز 'honored/proud' + بودی 'you were' (2nd person past). به هر انجمن is a locative/occasional prepositional phrase 'in/at every assembly' (به marks the occasion/location, هر 'every', انجمن 'assembly'). Together: 'you were honored at every assembly.'
segments.s271-g9: This is an elliptical, verbless clause typical of Persian poetry. ترا (here split into ت + را) functions as the second‑person object/pronoun ('you' / 'your'), بیشتر نزد من is an adverbial phrase meaning 'more/in my estimation', and دستگاه ('court, retinue, establishment') is the noun being evaluated. The linking verb 'is' is omitted but understood.
segments.s271-g10: توی = 'among' (indicates the comparison domain), برتر از is the comparative construction 'superior to', and پهلوانان is the plural 'champions/heroes'; به جاه is a prepositional phrase 'in rank/status' that marks the dimension of superiority. The clause states the standard of comparison and the aspect (rank) in which the addressee excels.
segments.s271-g11: A nominal clause with no overt verb: همیشه ('always') modifies the whole phrase, یکی جوشنی literally 'one (a certain) mail‑clad [man]' uses جوشنی (coat of mail) by synecdoche to mean a man in mail, and پیش من means 'before me / in my presence'.
segments.s271-g12: Two parallel participial/verb phrases using کرده (perfective 'has done') after nouns: سپر کرده جان = 'has made (his) life a shield' (سپر as object, کرده as light perfective), and فدی کرده تن = 'has sacrificed/offered (as) ransom his body' (فدی here means 'sacrifice/ransom'). The sequence expresses self‑sacrificial action; the subject is the mail‑clad man from the previous segment.
segments.s271-g13: همیدون is a proper name (subject). The phrases به هر کار ('in/for every affair') and با گنج خویش ('with his treasure/wealth') are adverbial/instrumental modifiers describing the scope and means of his actions; خوی + ش together form خویش 'his own'.
segments.s271-g14: گزیده is a past participle / perfect meaning 'has chosen' (here functioning as the main verb). ز بهر = 'for the sake of', منی is an enclitic form meaning 'me' / 'my sake', and رنج خویش = 'his own suffering' (رنج = suffering; خویش = his own). The clause says he elected to endure hardship on the speaker's behalf.
segments.s271-g15: Standard past‑tense clause: تو (you) is subject, بردی is the simple past 'you took/led/carried', ز چین indicates origin 'from China', تا ... به ایران 'as far as to Iran' marks the extent/destination, and سپاه (army) is the object; word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect but the verb is second person past.
segments.s271-g16: تو کردی is another past clause 'you made/did'; دل و بخت دشمن is the compound object 'the enemy's heart and fortune', and سیاه is a predicate adjective 'black/dark' describing the state inflicted on them. کردنی + object + adjective is a causative/resultative pattern: 'you caused X to be Y.'

sentences.s271-s69: And after that, from Iran the army’s trumpet rose — the blast of the horn and the Indian drum.
sentences.s271-s57: A fresh flower springs forth from the dry thorn; the earth, with awakened fortune, turns to musk.
sentences.s271-s58: More astonishing is that a man, pursuing greed, always holds his heart in sorrow.
sentences.s271-s59: Between ancestor and descendant — two kings — I do not know why there should be this seat of hatred.
sentences.s271-s60: Two kings and two realms — such warriors — have brought two hosts face to face.
sentences.s271-s29: May no one's eye behold that time when he takes the initiative in the affair.
sentences.s271-s30: For I myself am resolved that from here I will lead an army across to the far side of the Jeyhun by the road.
sentences.s271-s31: I am neither Goudarz nor Khosrow nor Tous, nor am I time, crown, trumpet, or drum.
sentences.s271-s32: I will lead an army into Iran in such a manner that thereafter no one will see crown or throne.
sentences.s271-s9: No host has seen a commander like you; no one girds his loins like you, alert as you are.
sentences.s271-s10: If from Tur and Pashang affection arises, then for a hero like you even the heavens will emit spikenard.
sentences.s271-s11: First, that which you said in the assembly: I always hold myself a sinner.
sentences.s271-s12: When Kai Khosrow came from the land of Turan to Iran, he spread enmity against us.
sentences.s271-s49: He gave his whole heart to the army, that the heart might be freed from sorrow.
sentences.s271-s50: Secretly his soul was full of pain.
sentences.s271-s51: For he saw the king's host diminishing on every side in the battle.
sentences.s271-s52: Also his heart was tight because of King Khosrow; he feared that one by one they would come to battle.
sentences.s271-s41: When the army comes near you, do not stir — tear Goudarz’s head and crown from their place.
sentences.s271-s42: That very mountain where he has made a fortress — unhorse them: bring the war-horses down from beneath their feet.
sentences.s271-s43: Do not withhold your hand from shedding their blood; may you be victorious by hanging (them).
sentences.s271-s44: Spare no one of them alive in the world who comes near you — there are many of them.
sentences.s271-s37: From men, from treasure, and from the strength of hand — all divine things, whatever you need, are here.
sentences.s271-s38: One renowned host, an army of ten thousand, brave and wise, both foot and horsemen.
sentences.s271-s39: I have sent them now to your vicinity so that they may illumine your dark soul.
sentences.s271-s40: Of the Iranians there are ten, while of these (men) only one; to the eye, one seems like ten horsemen — (yet they are) few.
sentences.s271-s53: He said to God thus: "O Creator, what a marvel is this in these days?"
sentences.s271-s54: Whom have you exalted? None is cast down; there is no sovereign or possessor except you.
sentences.s271-s55: Look at Khosrow; for who, apart from the Creator, knew that a single king would come?
sentences.s271-s56: Look — behold this deed, O turner of the age; tell me that which he did for his own sake.
sentences.s271-s61: What do you say will be the ultimate outcome of this battle — whom will the turning of fate strike down?
sentences.s271-s62: Then he wailed bitterly to God, crying: 'O luminous, just Creator!'
sentences.s271-s63: If Afrasiab, in this den of hatred, is reluctant, (even) the famed troops of Turan...
sentences.s271-s64: In this battlefield we shall be slain; our pride/fame will be undone.
sentences.s271-s45: The messenger heard the king's message and came to the champion of the army.
sentences.s271-s46: He came forward into their presence like a sorcerer, bent as if under the weight of a jasmine bough.
sentences.s271-s47: He delivered the king's message to the elders, and to those famed warlike men of the host.
sentences.s271-s48: When the elders heard, they summoned the army; the messenger, when he heard these words, turned and drove back.
sentences.s271-s21: The business of battle is always like this: from every side this dark turning occurs.
sentences.s271-s22: Sometimes it lifts up to the sun’s head; sometimes it draws back from the sun.
sentences.s271-s23: The lofty sky does not remain the same: sometimes it grants joy, sometimes it makes one needy.
sentences.s271-s24: Sometimes it goes with wine and with entertainers; sometimes with sorrow and ardor and with thoughts.
sentences.s271-s65: When Kay Khosrow comes from Iran to take vengeance, the whole land will be restored to him.
sentences.s271-s66: It would be fitting that, if I grow weary in my cuirass, the Creator should take my soul from my body.
sentences.s271-s67: May no worldly-seeing person ever perceive that anyone has seized my way and my custom.
sentences.s271-s68: For whoever the course of the day is not in accordance with his desire, for him life and death are one.
sentences.s271-s33: I will no longer remain in this world for Kay Khosrow; suddenly I will come down upon his head.
sentences.s271-s34: With a dagger I will cut off his head in that manner, so that his army and country will weep for him.
sentences.s271-s35: Unless some other heavenly affair should come forth from the turning of fate.
sentences.s271-s36: O worldly‑wise, high‑exalted one, God has not made you in need of anything.
sentences.s271-s17: I am not Hemidun; do not bring him to me — I will not seek the elixir in these words.
sentences.s271-s18: In this matter he is not guilty; I have no quarrel with the sovereign.
sentences.s271-s19: So it was; and such being was the work — what hurt was there in my heart because of you?
sentences.s271-s20: And besides, when you spoke of the matter of the host — of the sun and moon’s becoming dark.
sentences.s271-s13: I, who am king, have not been brought to supplication in this way; in my heart I have never admitted this remembrance.
sentences.s271-s14: You should not be so narrow‑hearted; from care the heart's alarm will find you.
sentences.s271-s15: That which exists was from the Creator; no evil teacher came in this way.
sentences.s271-s16: Lest Keykhosrow take the radiance from me; do not call him an heir — that would be a lie.
sentences.s271-s25: Do not weary your heart with this pain; do not bind your soul to this affair.
sentences.s271-s26: Talking of the slain turned into sleep; because of a brother's hatred, do not lift your head to strike.
sentences.s271-s27: A heart that, from the pain on account of a brother, has ruined itself—physicians' remedies bring it no benefit.
sentences.s271-s28: Three others that you said: that Khosrow, at dawn, will enter into battle with an army.
sentences.s271-s1: When he set the night‑cap on his head like a crown, at that very moment he opened the door to the messenger.
sentences.s271-s2: He commanded that he return to his place, toward the famed servant of the village‑headman.
sentences.s271-s3: He answered thus: 'Say this — O kind, good‑hearted, truthful one,'
sentences.s271-s4: You — being of noble birth from a pure mother — were honored in every assembly.
sentences.s271-s5: To me you are more esteemed: your court (retinue) is superior in rank to the champions.
sentences.s271-s6: There is always one mail-clad man before me; he has made his life a shield and has offered his body as a ransom/sacrifice.
sentences.s271-s7: Hamīdūn, in every enterprise with his treasure, has chosen (to bear) his own suffering for my sake.
sentences.s271-s8: You led an army from China to (as far as) Iran; you made the enemy's heart and fortune dark.
